advantages: Variance names the overfitting mechanism: unstable predictions across training samples, not just 'bad accuracy'.
The bias² + variance + noise decomposition guides concrete fixes—more data, regularization, or simpler models when variance dominates.
Variance-aware model choice explains why ensembles and cross-validation work in practice, not only in theory.
Separating variance from bias prevents misdiagnosing underfitting (high bias, low variance) as overfitting and vice versa.
disadvantages: Variance is defined over an ideal distribution of training sets you never fully observe—you estimate it indirectly via CV or bootstrap.
The classic decomposition is cleanest for squared error; classification and ranking metrics need different or approximate decompositions.
Reducing variance can raise bias; blindly simplifying models may underfit even when validation error drops slightly.
Deep models can have low bias and controlled variance via regularization, breaking naive 'more parameters always mean more variance' intuition on large data.
